title: Backend and Data Interview Question Bank
subtitle: Intermediate backend questions about APIs, persistence, validation, and reliability.

questions.question: Why should request validation happen near the API boundary?
questions.intent: Checks whether you isolate bad input early.
options: A. To reject invalid payloads before deeper business logic runs.
B. To make every request slower.
C. Because databases cannot store strings.
questions.correct_answer: A. To reject invalid payloads before deeper business logic runs.
questions.explanation: Boundary validation reduces downstream error states, keeps contracts explicit, and prevents invalid data from leaking into core logic or persistence layers.
questions.code_snippet: if not payload.get('email'):\n    raise ValueError('email is required')
compare: Early validation: clearer failure and safer internal code.
Late validation: more branching and harder debugging.

questions.question: What is a transaction and why is it useful?
questions.intent: Checks whether you understand atomic multi-step changes.
options: A. A transaction groups operations so they succeed or fail together.
B. A transaction is only a SQL comment.
C. A transaction guarantees infinite scaling.
questions.correct_answer: A. A transaction groups operations so they succeed or fail together.
questions.explanation: Transactions help preserve data consistency. If one step fails, the system can roll back the whole unit of work instead of leaving partial writes.
compare: Transaction: safer for coupled writes.
No transaction: partial state is more likely on failure.

questions.question: When does caching help a backend service?
questions.intent: Tests whether you know why and when to trade freshness for speed.
options: A. When repeated expensive reads can tolerate some staleness.
B. For every write-heavy workflow automatically.
C. Only when the database is broken.
questions.correct_answer: A. When repeated expensive reads can tolerate some staleness.
questions.explanation: Caching reduces latency and backend load, but it introduces invalidation and freshness tradeoffs. It helps most when the same data is read often and changes less frequently.
questions.code_snippet:
cached = cache.get(key)
if cached is not None:
    return cached
value = load_from_db(key)
cache.set(key, value, ttl=60)
compare: Cache hit: lower latency and lower DB load.
Cache miss or stale cache: extra complexity and potential inconsistency.

questions.question: Why can ORM convenience still require SQL knowledge?
questions.intent: Checks whether you understand abstractions do not remove query cost.
options: A. ORM-generated queries can still be inefficient or incorrect for the workload.
B. ORMs replace all need for data modeling.
C. SQL is unrelated to relational databases.
questions.correct_answer: A. ORM-generated queries can still be inefficient or incorrect for the workload.
questions.explanation: You still need to understand joins, indexes, and query shape. Otherwise, an ORM can hide slow N+1 queries or pull much more data than needed.
questions.code_snippet: users = session.query(User).all()\nfor user in users:\n    print(user.orders)  # may trigger N+1 queries
compare: ORM: faster development and cleaner models.
Raw SQL insight: necessary for performance and debugging.

questions.question: Why should external API failures be isolated from core domain logic?
questions.intent: Tests whether you can limit blast radius.
options: A. So upstream instability does not crash unrelated business flows.
B. So the codebase has more folders.
C. Because external APIs are always optional.
questions.correct_answer: A. So upstream instability does not crash unrelated business flows.
questions.explanation: Separating providers behind an adapter or service boundary makes timeouts, retries, fallbacks, and test doubles easier to manage without contaminating the rest of the system.
compare: Isolated adapter: easier testing and controlled retries.
Provider logic everywhere: harder to change and debug.

questions.question: Which is usually a backend anti-pattern for loading related data: one query per parent row or one shaped query that fetches needed relations eagerly?
options: A. One query per parent row is the anti-pattern because it often creates N+1 queries.
B. The shaped query is always slower by definition.
C. Databases optimize both into identical execution plans automatically.
questions.correct_answer: A. One query per parent row is the anti-pattern because it often creates N+1 queries.
questions.explanation: Repeated relation loading causes avoidable round trips and query overhead. Loading strategy should match the actual access pattern.
compare: N+1 loading: simple to write, expensive at scale.
Planned eager loading: often better for known access paths.

questions.question: What is the performance trap in selecting `*` from a large table when only two fields are needed?
options: A. Extra columns increase transfer, parsing, and memory cost.
B. SQL does not allow selecting fewer fields in production.
C. Wide rows always compress to the same size anyway.
questions.correct_answer: A. Extra columns increase transfer, parsing, and memory cost.
questions.explanation: Selecting only the required columns is a simple optimization that can matter significantly on large datasets or hot endpoints.
questions.code_snippet: SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE status = 'active';
compare: SELECT *: convenient but often wasteful.
Projected columns: leaner for bandwidth and memory.

questions.question: Why is caching mutable database-derived objects and then mutating them in-place a trap?
options: A. Other requests may observe stale or unexpectedly modified cached state.
B. Python automatically deep-copies all cached values.
C. Cached objects cannot be mutated after retrieval.
questions.correct_answer: A. Other requests may observe stale or unexpectedly modified cached state.
questions.explanation: Caches need careful control over object identity, mutation, serialization, and invalidation.
questions.code_snippet: profile = cache.get(key)\nprofile['count'] += 1  # risky if shared mutable cache object
compare: Mutable shared cache objects: risky and surprising.
Immutable/serialized cache values: safer boundaries.

questions.question: Why is returning raw internal exceptions directly to API clients a bad idea?
options: A. It can leak internals and produce unstable client-facing contracts.
B. Clients prefer stack traces for all failures.
C. It makes performance benchmarking easier.
questions.correct_answer: A. It can leak internals and produce unstable client-facing contracts.
questions.explanation: Client-facing errors should be deliberate, stable, and safe rather than accidental reflections of internal implementation.
compare: Raw internal exception: unstable and potentially unsafe.
Mapped API error contract: cleaner for clients.

questions.question: What is the trap in checking uniqueness only in application code before insert and not enforcing it in the database?
options: A. Concurrent requests can still insert duplicates.
B. Databases reject duplicates automatically without constraints.
C. Application checks are always stronger than constraints.
questions.correct_answer: A. Concurrent requests can still insert duplicates.
questions.explanation: Application checks alone are not enough under concurrency. Important uniqueness should usually be enforced in the database as well.
questions.code_snippet: ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT users_email_key UNIQUE (email);
compare: App-only uniqueness check: race-prone.
Database constraint: stronger integrity protection.

questions.question: Which is usually safer in request-scoped code: one long-lived global session or a controlled session per unit of work?
options: A. A controlled session per unit of work is usually safer.
B. One global session is always best because it saves memory.
C. ORM sessions never hold state between operations.
questions.correct_answer: A. A controlled session per unit of work is usually safer.
questions.explanation: Global long-lived sessions can accumulate stale state, transaction confusion, and cross-request leakage.
compare: Scoped session: clearer lifecycle and isolation.
Global session: convenient at first, fragile later.

questions.question: Why is cache invalidation hard in backend systems?
options: A. Because you must balance freshness, correctness, and efficiency.
B. Because caches delete themselves correctly by default.
C. Because invalidation only matters in frontend apps.
questions.correct_answer: A. Because you must balance freshness, correctness, and efficiency.
questions.explanation: The hard part of caching is not storing values, but keeping them accurate enough for real product behavior.
compare: No invalidation strategy: stale data risk.
Deliberate invalidation: more complexity, more correctness.

questions.question: Which is usually a better optimization target first for a slow endpoint: tiny syntax tweaks or measuring query count and latency sources?
options: A. Measure query count and latency sources first.
B. Micro-optimize variable names and indentation first.
C. Add more comments before measuring anything.
questions.correct_answer: A. Measure query count and latency sources first.
questions.explanation: Performance work should start with evidence. Real bottlenecks are often I/O, query shape, or repeated remote calls, not tiny syntax choices.
compare: Evidence-driven tuning: better ROI.
Blind micro-optimization: low confidence and often wasted effort.

questions.question: Why can coupling an HTTP request directly to a slow external provider hurt backend reliability?
options: A. Provider slowness can tie up request latency and resources end-to-end.
B. HTTP servers automatically turn slow providers into async queues.
C. Slow providers only affect frontend rendering.
questions.correct_answer: A. Provider slowness can tie up request latency and resources end-to-end.
questions.explanation: Sometimes the better design is a background job, timeout, fallback, cache, or asynchronous workflow rather than a synchronous dependency on the hot path.
compare: Synchronous dependency on hot path: simpler, but riskier.
Async or buffered design: more resilient under slow dependencies.
